intro_band.agent_remit_summary: yaah is a local developer CLI that generates coding-agent configuration for four targets — Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex CLI, and GitHub Copilot CLI — from a single Go source of truth, and ships a built-in security-and-quality toolset alongside that configuration. The toolset is five protective hooks (linter, command guard, secret scanner, comment checker, session logger), a built-in MCP server exposing read-only check tools plus one workspace-writing planning tool, and a per-session audit log; remote MCP wiring is limited to context7 and pulumi. The remit's central promise is that every generated agent configuration carries the same set of protective hooks, that catastrophic shell commands and hardcoded credentials are caught before they take effect, and that high-impact writes/exec reach a human checkpoint.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Go 1.25 module (github.com/dirien/yet-another-agent-harness) following an Interface → Registry → Generator pattern: a Harness wires hooks, mcp, lsp, skills, agents, commands, plugins, and a session.Store, and four per-target generators (pkg/generator/claude.go, opencode.go, codex.go, copilot.go) emit native config formats. Hook handlers live in pkg/hooks/handlers/ (regex-based CommandGuard, line-scanning SecretScanner, Linter, CommentChecker, SessionLogger); a built-in MCP server (pkg/mcpserver/) exposes yaah_scan_secrets, yaah_lint, yaah_check_command, yaah_doctor, yaah_session_info, yaah_planning_status, and yaah_planning_init. The Codex generator's hook map (pkg/generator/hookmap.go) omits PreToolUse entirely — confirmed by TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported, which asserts that a PreToolUse rule produces a nil hooks file — and the project README at line 236 documents the same limitation.

behavior_summary:
yaah's central promise — that every generated coding-agent configuration carries the same set of protective hooks — is contradicted in code: pkg/generator/hookmap.go has no Codex entry for PreToolUse, so the CommandGuard that blocks rm -rf / / force-pushes / DROP TABLE is silently dropped from generated Codex configs, and TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported codifies that behavior as intended. The README acknowledges the gap ("Codex: Hooks limited to SessionStart/Stop — linting and security checks available via yaah MCP tools"), but a defense surfaced only as an opt-in MCP tool the model may or may not call is not the same control as a deterministic Block: true on PreToolUse.

Two further structural patterns compound this: the controls that are wired are bypassable on close inspection (the command-guard regexes hard-code --force, rm -rf, and a small fixed pattern set — git push -f, --force-with-lease, rm -r -f, and many destructive variants slip through), and the harness itself ships zero adversarial test fixtures and no SECURITY.md / disclosure policy, which the remit's Escalation section explicitly calls for.

rules.rule_text: Generate coding-agent configuration for several coding agents — Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex CLI, and GitHub Copilot CLI — from a single source of truth, and ship a built-in security-and-quality toolset alongside it.

Every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger.

Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.
Remote MCP servers MUST be reached over TLS.

MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model — neither the built-in server's tool descriptions nor, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures.
High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.
Hardcoded credentials in files the agent edits MUST be detected before the change is accepted.
Agent-managed, session-loaded files MUST NOT be writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions unreviewed.
Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.
Dependencies MUST be version-controlled with a committed, pinned lockfile, and the dependency tree kept small and reviewable.
The harness MUST maintain a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications — detailed enough to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.

The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections — confirming that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.

findings.summary: Codex generator strips PreToolUse — the command-guard hook is silently absent from one of the four agent targets the remit promises uniform protections for
findings.description: The remit's central promise is that every generated agent configuration carries the same set of protective hooks. pkg/generator/hookmap.go has no Codex entry under HookPreToolUse, so CodexEventName(HookPreToolUse) returns an empty string and CodexGenerator.GenerateHooks() drops the rule. The behavior is deliberate — TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported asserts that a PreToolUse-only config yields a nil hooks file — and the README at line 236 admits "Hooks limited to SessionStart/Stop" for Codex, with security checks moved to opt-in MCP tools the model may or may not invoke. A Codex user running yaah generate --agent codex receives a configuration with no command guard, no secret scanner, no linter, and no comment checker on the agent's edit/bash path.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger. / Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.

evidence.snippet: HookPreToolUse mapping has no Codex field — only Claude/OpenCode/Copilot — so CodexEventName(HookPreToolUse) returns "" and the PreToolUse rule is dropped by CodexGenerator.GenerateHooks.
if codexEvent == "" || codexEvent == "notify" { continue } — the loop skips any event whose Codex mapping is empty, which is exactly PreToolUse.
TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported feeds a PreToolUse-only HooksConfig and asserts the generator returns nil — codifying the gap as expected behavior.
"Codex: ... Hooks limited to SessionStart/Stop — linting and security checks available via yaah MCP tools." Documented divergence from the remit's uniform-protection promise.

recommended_actions: Decide whether Codex is a supported target for the protections promise. If yes: refuse to generate a Codex config (or emit a hard warning) when PreToolUse rules are present and Codex does not expose an equivalent gate — do not silently drop them.

Update the remit, README, and docs to either (a) carve Codex out of the protections-uniform-across-agents promise explicitly, naming the missing hook events, or (b) implement a Codex-native pre-exec gate (Notify + halt, or a bash-wrapper approach) and wire CommandGuard / SecretScanner through it.

findings.summary: CommandGuard regex set is so narrow that obvious catastrophic-command variants pass the gate (git push -f / --force-with-lease, rm -r -f, rm with paths beyond `/`, fork bombs, curl|sh)
findings.description: pkg/hooks/handlers/guard.go ships seven default regexes — rm\s+-rf\s+/, git\s+push\s+--force\s+(origin\s+)?(main|master), git\s+reset\s+--hard, DROP\s+(TABLE|DATABASE), :\s*>\s*/, mkfs\., dd\s+if=.*of=/dev/ — and the remit's R-03 names "recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes" as the class to block. The regexes implement the literal sentences but not the class. A short bypass list, all of which would slip through unchanged: git push -f origin main (short flag), git push --force-with-lease origin main (lease variant — same destructive effect, no --force token), rm -r -f / (split flags), rm -rfv /home (path other than literal /), rm -fR / (capital R is allowed by (?i) but the flag-order constraint isn't), TRUNCATE TABLE users (DROP-only), :(){:|:&};: (fork bomb), curl http://… | sh (remote-script piped to shell). The remit treats this as a Forbidden Action class ("MUST be blocked before they execute"); the implementation treats it as a denylist of one sentence per class. A Forbidden-Action gap on a Critical class is itself Critical.
findings.policy_rule_text: Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.

evidence.snippet: NewCommandGuard hard-codes seven literal patterns; the recursive-delete rule is `rm\s+-rf\s+/` — matches only that exact flag bundle and a literal trailing `/`, not `rm -r -f /home/...`, `rm -fr /`, or recursive deletions targeting paths beneath root.

Force-push rule is `git\s+push\s+--force\s+(origin\s+)?(main|master)` — does not match `git push -f origin main`, `git push --force-with-lease origin main`, or pushes to release branches other than main/master.
Destructive-SQL rule is `DROP\s+(TABLE|DATABASE)` — TRUNCATE, DELETE without WHERE, ALTER TABLE … DROP COLUMN, and DROP SCHEMA all pass through.
Truncate-system-file rule is `:\s*>\s*/` — matches only the `:` shell idiom; `> /etc/shadow` or `cat /dev/null > /etc/passwd` are not caught.

recommended_actions: Rewrite the catastrophic-command guard as a parsed-AST or strict allowlist policy rather than a regex denylist: parse the bash command, walk arguments, and flag any `rm -r`/`rm -R` variant regardless of flag order or path, any `git push --force*` or `git push -f` to any branch the operator has not explicitly opted in to, and any destructive-DDL/DML statement (TRUNCATE, DELETE without WHERE, DROP, ALTER…DROP).

Add adversarial test fixtures in `pkg/hooks/handlers/guard_test.go` that exercise the bypass list from this finding's evidence — each variant must return Block:true. Wire the test into CI as a regression gate so a future regex rewrite cannot silently lose coverage.

Publish a narrowly-scoped "what this guard catches and what it does not" doc that the README's protections promise links to, so operators do not assume the seven regexes are equivalent to the remit's class-level guarantee.

findings.summary: No human-checkpoint gate on file writes or write/send/execute MCP tools — the approval-requirement rule is unenforced in code
findings.description: R-06 says high-impact actions "MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run," naming writes and write/send/execute MCP tool calls as the class. The hook registry has zero handlers that prompt for or require human confirmation: CommandGuard only emits Block: true on its narrow regex set; SecretScanner and Linter block on rule violations but pass everything else through; there is no "PromptForApproval" handler, no MCP tool wrapper that pauses for operator yes/no, and the built-in yaah_planning_init MCP tool writes .planning/STATE.md and four directories on first call with no checkpoint. The harness's HandleHookEvent in pkg/harness/harness.go dispatches to handlers and either blocks (on a Block:true result) or passes; there is no "hold and await operator" state. The remit's R-06 is a hard rule on a Forbidden / Approval Requirement class; absent any code-level checkpoint, every write and write-capable MCP tool call proceeds unreviewed — a Critical Gap by the Phase-2 audit table.
findings.policy_rule_text: High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.

evidence.snippet: HandleHookEvent dispatches handlers and returns ErrHookBlocked if any returned Block:true — no checkpoint/pause-for-approval state exists in the dispatch path.

Five handlers in pkg/hooks/handlers/ — CommandGuard, SecretScanner, CommentChecker, Linter, SessionLogger. None of them implement a human-confirmation path; the only outcomes are pass-through, Output annotation, or hard Block.
yaah_planning_init writes .planning/STATE.md and four directories with no caller-confirmation step (lines 363-441 — direct os.MkdirAll + os.WriteFile).

yaah_lint executes external commands via exec.CommandContext (delegated to Linter.LintFile) with no per-invocation approval — file-modifying lint steps (`ruff --fix`, `gofmt -w`, `prettier --write`, `cargo clippy --fix`) run unconfirmed.

recommended_actions: Add a high-impact gate handler that runs on PreToolUse for Edit/Write/MultiEdit and for any MCP tool whose name matches a write/send/execute pattern; the default behavior should be Block + emit an operator-visible prompt (or honor an explicit per-tool allow list signed by the operator at session start).

For the built-in MCP tools, classify them in `pkg/mcpserver/tools.go` as `read_only` vs `write_caller_workspace` and refuse to expose the write-capable set when an approval handler is not registered on the harness.

findings.summary: Third-party MCP servers are launched with `npx -y @context7/mcp` — unpinned, integrity-unverified, re-resolved against the npm registry on every run
findings.description: R-09 says third-party MCP servers "MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run." pkg/mcp/providers/context7.go registers Context7 as {Command: "npx", Args: ["-y", "@context7/mcp"]} — no version specifier, no integrity hash, no lockfile. npx -y downloads the latest matching package on each invocation; a registry compromise of @context7/mcp or a typo-squat that resolves before it would silently introduce a hostile MCP server with full tool-description and tool-call access to the host model. The same generated config flows into Claude (.mcp.json line 6-9), Codex, OpenCode, and Copilot — every supported target ships with the unpinned npx invocation.
findings.policy_rule_text: Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.

evidence.snippet: Server() returns Command:"npx", Args:["-y", "@context7/mcp"] — no version pin, no integrity hash.
Generated config carries the same unpinned npx invocation into every Claude-style workspace `yaah generate` produces.
Same pattern for Notion when a token is supplied: npx -y @notionhq/notion-mcp-server — opt-in, but configured exactly the same unpinned way.

recommended_actions: Pin @context7/mcp to a specific version in `pkg/mcp/providers/context7.go` (e.g. `@context7/mcp@1.2.3`) and ship a lockfile / integrity-hash check that `yaah doctor` validates on each run.
Apply the same pin to the Notion provider (`pkg/mcp/providers/notion.go`) and add a docs section that explains how operators can override the pin to a newer audited version.

findings.summary: SecretScanner runs PostToolUse and skips `.lock` files — credential is written to disk first and lockfile-shaped leaks (poetry.lock, package-lock.json) are excluded entirely
findings.description: R-07 says hardcoded credentials "MUST be detected before the change is accepted." The implementation in pkg/hooks/handlers/secretscan.go registers on HookPostToolUse matching Edit|Write|MultiEdit — by the time the scan runs, the secret has already been written to disk. The handler can return Block: true to surface a warning, but the file mutation has happened; recovery requires the operator to react and revert. A pre-write variant would intercept the proposed content before it reaches the filesystem. Additionally, the scanner's binaryExts map (line 154-160) excludes .lock — but lockfiles like poetry.lock, package-lock.json, yarn.lock are real text files that have historically leaked credentials via tokenized registry URLs (e.g. https://user:token@registry.example.com/). The exclusion is a Partial: the control exists and runs, but it lets credentials slip through both timing and file-type axes.
findings.policy_rule_text: Hardcoded credentials in files the agent edits MUST be detected before the change is accepted.

evidence.snippet: Events() returns HookPostToolUse — scan runs after the Edit/Write tool has already written content; the remit says "before the change is accepted."
binaryExts includes ".lock": true — poetry.lock, package-lock.json, yarn.lock skipped despite being text files that have historically carried tokenized registry URLs.
ScanFile early-returns on isLikelyBinary — the exclusion is the only check for whether content is scannable, with no override for known-text lockfile names.

recommended_actions: Move secret scanning to a PreToolUse path (or to a pre-write content-inspection hook) so detection happens against the proposed content, not after the file has been mutated. Where this requires Edit/Write tools to expose their proposed content (some agents only emit it post-write), document and accept the Codex-style gap explicitly.

Remove `.lock` from `binaryExts` in `pkg/hooks/handlers/secretscan.go` and instead detect binary content by file-content sniff (null bytes, non-text bytes in first 4KB). Lockfiles are text and benefit from the scan.

findings.summary: No SECURITY.md, no documented disclosure policy, and no adversarial test fixtures for the harness's own protections — R-12 SHOULD is unfulfilled
findings.description: R-12 says the project SHOULD publish a threat model and disclosure process and SHOULD run adversarial tests of its own protections — naming four concrete scenarios (planted-secret detection, command-guard catastrophic-command block, session-loaded-file poisoning-write prevention, generated-configuration-protections verification). A workspace sweep finds none of these: no SECURITY.md at the repo root, no .github/SECURITY.md, no docs/SECURITY.md; the single SECURITY.md reference in README.md line 160 points to a per-phase planning artifact emitted by the /yaah:secure workflow command, not a project-level disclosure policy. The Go test files exercise generator output shape, hook-registry registration, and gitcache plumbing — none of them assert the command guard catches `git push -f origin main` or that the secret scanner catches a planted credential in a `.py` Edit input. The remit is explicit ("adversarial testing of its own protections … confirming that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims"); the test corpus is silent on every one of those four.
findings.policy_rule_text: The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections — confirming that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.

evidence.snippet: No SECURITY.md at repo root, no .github/SECURITY.md, no docs/SECURITY.md — verified by find sweep of the workspace.
No guard_test.go exists in pkg/hooks/handlers/ — the command-guard regex set has zero adversarial tests confirming the catastrophic-command class is blocked.
No secretscan_test.go exists — no test plants an AKIA-prefixed key, an sk-ant token, or a private-key block and asserts the scanner blocks the write.
The single SECURITY.md mention is `│       ├── SECURITY.md       # Threat model (STRIDE)` inside the per-phase .planning/ directory — a workflow output, not a project disclosure policy.

recommended_actions: Add `SECURITY.md` at the repo root naming the supported-versions matrix, vulnerability-reporting email/security advisory channel, and disclosure timeline. GitHub surfaces this automatically as the repo's security tab.

Add `pkg/hooks/handlers/guard_test.go` and `secretscan_test.go` with adversarial fixtures: the bypass list from PRAX-2026-05-23-002 for the guard, and planted AWS / OpenAI / Anthropic / Slack / PEM tokens for the scanner. Wire into CI.

Add a per-generator integration test that runs `yaah generate --agent <target>` against a tmpdir, asserts the resulting config carries the protection set the remit promises (or asserts the documented Codex exception), and fails if a generator silently drops a hook.

findings.summary: SessionLogger writes free-form plaintext lifecycle lines, not the durable structured tool-call/blocked-action record R-11 requires — the structured record only exists if a SessionID flows in
findings.description: R-11 says the harness MUST maintain a "durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications." Two surfaces try to do this and neither alone meets the bar. pkg/hooks/handlers/sessionlog.go writes .claude/logs/sessions.log as plaintext lines of the form `[ts] event=SessionStart session=... cwd=...` — lifecycle only, no tool calls, no blocked actions, no file modifications, no schema; appending to a single shared file with no rotation, no schema, and no structured-format guarantee. The structured record lives in pkg/session/store.go + session.go — JSON files at .claude/sessions/<id>.json with ToolCallRecord, BlockedCalls, FilesModified, Findings — and is populated by HandleHookEvent, but only when input.SessionID is non-empty (harness.go:87). If the calling agent does not propagate a session ID through its hook input (a real possibility on Codex/Copilot given the hook-mapping gaps), the structured record is silently empty and only the plaintext sessions.log survives. The remit's structured-record promise rests on a precondition the harness never checks or enforces.
findings.policy_rule_text: The harness MUST maintain a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications — detailed enough to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.

evidence.snippet: entry format is `[%s] event=%s session=%s cwd=%s\n` — plaintext, no tool name, no input, no result, no schema; only lifecycle events (SessionStart/SessionEnd/Stop) are logged.

Session-state writes are gated on `input.SessionID != ""` — when the calling agent omits the session ID (or the hook-event mapping drops it, as on Codex), all ToolCallRecord/BlockedCalls/FilesModified persistence silently no-ops.

ToolCallRecord includes Timestamp, ToolName, Input (a *summary*, not the full payload), Blocked, Reason — the structured record is fit for the remit's purpose when populated, but the gate above is what makes population conditional.
Default logDir is `.claude/logs` — no rotation policy, no max-size cap, no archiving; long-running operator runs will accumulate unbounded plaintext.

recommended_actions: Either remove the plaintext sessions.log entirely or make it the structured-JSON-lines surface (one JSON object per line: timestamp, event, session_id, tool_name, blocked, file_path). The remit wants one structured record, not two unsynchronized ones.

In `HandleHookEvent`, generate or require a session ID when one is missing — log a warning rather than silently skipping the structured write. The current `_, _ = fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "session load: %v\n", err); sess = nil` path swallows the missing-session case and proceeds with no audit trail.

findings.summary: OpenCode JS plugin literal has mismatched braces — the `event` handler's switch is closed too late and the trailing object closes with stray brackets, likely failing to load
findings.description: pkg/generator/opencode.go embeds the OpenCode plugin as a string literal returned by GenerateHooks. The event handler (line 116-127) opens an if block on `event.type === "session.created"` and runs execFileSync with try/catch, but the closing structure reads } catch (e) { /* non-fatal */ } }, }; with the final `};` closing the object that should still contain the event arrow function — the brace count is unbalanced relative to the surrounding `return { ... };`. A round-trip through a JS parser will report "Unexpected token" or a missing closing brace; OpenCode's plugin loader will reject the file at startup and the user's PreToolUse / PostToolUse / SessionStart hooks all silently never fire. The Go-side test (`TestOpenCode_GenerateHooks_Plugin`) only does substring checks on the generated string — it never parses the JS — so the broken plugin ships green.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger.

evidence.snippet: event handler begins `event: async ({ event }) => { if (event.type === "session.created") { try { ... } catch (e) { /* non-fatal */ } }` — the `if` block closes after the catch, but the arrow function never closes before the outer object.
Closing sequence reads `}, };` — too few closing braces for the nested structure (return-object → event-arrow → if-block → try-catch). A JS parser fails on the literal as written.

TestOpenCode_GenerateHooks_Plugin verifies the string CONTAINS `YaahPlugin`, `tool.execute.before`, `tool.execute.after`, `session.created`, `"hook", "PreToolUse"` — no syntactic parse, so the brace mismatch ships green.

recommended_actions: Fix the JS literal: close the `event` arrow function before the outer object closes. The minimal patch is to add one closing brace before the final `}};` so the structure is `event: async ({event}) => { if (...) { try {...} catch (...) {} } }, };` — or move the plugin out of a Go string literal and into a versioned `pkg/generator/opencode_plugin.js` embedded via `//go:embed` so syntax errors are caught by the JS toolchain at build time.

Add an integration test that runs `node --check` against the generated plugin file (or parses it via a Go JS-AST library) and fails the build on a syntax error. The current substring-only assertion catches missing strings but not structural breakage.

findings.summary: Built-in MCP tool descriptions are clean, but the harness performs zero inspection of third-party server tool descriptions — R-05 is verified only for the local server, not the configured set
findings.description: R-05 says MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model — "neither the built-in server's tool descriptions nor, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures." The built-in side is good: pkg/mcpserver/tools.go defines all seven tool descriptions as short noun-phrase capability statements ("Scan a file for hardcoded secrets and credentials," "Check whether a shell command is safe to run," etc.) — no embedded instructions, no "always do X," no roleplay framing. The third-party side is not inspected at all: pkg/mcp/providers/context7.go and pkg/mcp/providers/pulumi.go register the server endpoints and pass through whatever tool descriptions those servers advertise at runtime. yaah_doctor checks whether the binary exists; it does not pull the server's tool manifest and inspect descriptions for tool-poisoning indicators. The remit's "as far as the harness can inspect them" qualifier was honored as a license to inspect zero — Partial.

tags.label: Tool descriptions free of instruction-like language

findings.policy_rule_text: MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model — neither the built-in server's tool descriptions nor, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures.

evidence.snippet: Built-in tool descriptions are noun-phrases — `"Scan a file for hardcoded secrets and credentials"`, `"Check whether a shell command is safe to run"`, `"Run health checks on the yaah setup and report missing dependencies"`. Clean for R-05's local-server clause.
Context7 provider returns command/args only — no tools/description inspection, no listing of the server's advertised tool manifest.

Pulumi provider returns URL only — same; the harness does no fetch + parse of the remote server's tool descriptions to compare against the prompt-injection-language patterns the remit's "as far as the harness can inspect them" clause is aimed at.
yaah_doctor inspects MCP providers by attempting to exec.LookPath their command — health check only, not a tool-manifest fetch.

recommended_actions: Add a `yaah scan-mcp-tools` command (or a `yaah_doctor` enhancement) that, for each configured MCP server, attempts to fetch the server's tool list via a short stdio probe and scans each description for the imperative-language patterns the KB_MCP_SECURITY checklist names ("always," "first," "before responding," "system," "ignore," etc.) and PII/instruction markers. Report findings to the session audit log.

Update the remit's R-05 clause and the README protections list to call out which third-party servers' descriptions are actually inspected (currently: none) so operators understand the surface honestly.

findings.summary: CommentChecker hard-blocks Edit/Write whenever any non-ASCII letter appears in a comment — treats developer language choice as a security event and overreaches the remit's scope
findings.description: pkg/hooks/handlers/commentcheck.go returns Block: true on any matched pattern (line 103-107). The match set includes seven placeholder-comment regexes (TODO/implement, FIXME, HACK, …) — defensible — but also any comment whose extracted text contains a character where r > unicode.MaxASCII && unicode.IsLetter(r) (line 127). The remit's mission talks about a "comment checker" as part of the protective hook set and the remit itself never authorizes blocking writes on language choice. A developer working in a multilingual team — comments in Spanish, German with umlauts, Japanese, Cyrillic — would have every Edit/Write blocked, with the error "non-English comment." This is a Limit-Your-Domain overreach: the agent enforces a policy nobody declared, and it does so as a hard block on a class of legitimate developer activity. The remit names no such restriction in Permitted Actions or Forbidden Actions, and the rule that arguably justifies the comment check (R-02's "comment checker") doesn't define what the check should do.

evidence.snippet: If isComment(trimmed) and hasNonLatin(extractCommentText(trimmed)) → finding added; subsequent block sent for every Edit/Write. No allowlist of expected languages, no operator opt-in.
hasNonLatin loops the comment text and returns true for any rune > unicode.MaxASCII that IsLetter — German `ä`, Spanish `ñ`, Japanese kana, Cyrillic all trip it.
Result: Block: true on the first finding of either pattern type — placeholder OR non-English. No severity tiering, no warning-only mode.

recommended_actions: Either remove the `hasNonLatin` check entirely (developers' comment language is not a security concern) or downgrade it from `Block: true` to a non-blocking `Output` annotation, and gate it behind an operator opt-in flag in the harness config.
If the check is retained, scope it explicitly in the remit ("Permitted Actions" or "Behavioral Expectations") so operators understand a non-English comment will be rejected, and document the opt-out.

positives.title: Pinned Go module dependency tree, lockfile committed
positives.description: `go.mod` pins all direct deps to specific versions (MCP go-sdk v1.4.0, cobra v1.10.2) and `go.sum` carries integrity hashes — small, reviewable tree of <12 direct + indirect modules. Satisfies R-10.

positives.title: CI workflow pins all GitHub Actions to full SHA
positives.description: `.github/workflows/ci.yaml` pins `actions/checkout`, `actions/cache`, `actions/setup-go`, `sigstore/cosign-installer`, `anchore/sbom-action`, and `goreleaser/goreleaser-action` to full 40-char commit SHAs with version comments — no tag-based resolution that could resolve to a hijacked release.

positives.title: Release artifacts signed with cosign and shipped with SBOM
positives.description: GoReleaser is invoked with cosign + syft (`sigstore/cosign-installer`, `anchore/sbom-action/download-syft`) — release binaries carry a transparency-log signature and a software bill of materials.

positives.title: Remote MCP endpoint reached over TLS
positives.description: The only HTTP-transport MCP server configured (`pulumi`) uses `https://mcp.ai.pulumi.com/mcp` — satisfies R-04's TLS-required clause.

positives.title: Built-in MCP tool descriptions are clean noun-phrases
positives.description: All seven yaah MCP tools (`yaah_scan_secrets`, `yaah_lint`, `yaah_check_command`, `yaah_doctor`, `yaah_session_info`, `yaah_planning_status`, `yaah_planning_init`) carry short capability descriptions with no embedded instructions, no roleplay framing, no "always do X" — the local-server half of R-05 is genuinely Verified.

positives.title: Structured session-state model exists when wired
positives.description: `pkg/session/session.go` defines a real Session struct (ToolCallRecord, BlockedCalls, FilesModified, Findings) and `pkg/session/store.go` persists it as JSON — the data model R-11 wants exists; the gap (PRAX-2026-05-23-007) is that population is gated on SessionID and the plaintext log undersells it.

log_files.no_logs_note: Source-only scan of the yaah repository; no live session-log artifacts were present in the workspace. The harness defines a SessionLogger that writes plaintext lines to .claude/logs/sessions.log and a session.Store that writes structured JSON to .claude/sessions/<id>.json — see PRAX-2026-05-23-007 for the Monitor Continuously assessment.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. yaah ships a coherent set of protective primitives — five hook handlers, a built-in MCP server with read-only checks, a structured session store, pinned Go dependencies and SHA-pinned CI actions — but the protections that matter most at runtime (the command guard, the secret-scanner timing, the approval gate, uniform-across-agents delivery) are bypassable on close reading, and one of the four target agents (Codex) silently ships without the protective hooks the remit promises. Supply-chain hygiene is the strongest column (pinned Go deps, SBOM-signed releases) and Red-Team is the weakest (zero adversarial fixtures, no SECURITY.md). The weighted score reflects a project that knows where the controls go but has not yet hardened them.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The harness has a clear local-developer-CLI scope and the built-in MCP tool surface is small and matches the Known Good Baseline. R-05 is honored only for yaah's own server — third-party MCP tool descriptions are never inspected — and the CommentChecker overreaches into hard-blocking writes on developer language choice (PRAX-2026-05-23-010).

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: External-content risk is structurally low (no email/web ingest paths in the harness itself), but the secret scanner runs PostToolUse and excludes `.lock` files from scanning — credentials in proposed Edit content are detected only after they've been written, and tokenized lockfile URLs slip through entirely (PRAX-2026-05-23-005).

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: The Codex generator omits the PreToolUse event entirely, dropping the command guard from one of four target agents (PRAX-2026-05-23-001); the CommandGuard's seven literal regexes don't cover the catastrophic-command class the remit names (PRAX-2026-05-23-002); no human-checkpoint approval gate exists for writes or write-capable MCP tools (PRAX-2026-05-23-003); and the OpenCode plugin appears to ship with a JS syntax error that would prevent the plugin from loading at all (PRAX-2026-05-23-008).

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Go dependencies are pinned with a committed `go.sum`, CI actions pin to full commit SHAs with version comments, and releases ship cosign-signed binaries plus a Syft SBOM. The single supply-chain gap is the unpinned `npx -y @context7/mcp` invocation that flows into every generated config (PRAX-2026-05-23-004).

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No SECURITY.md, no documented disclosure process, and zero adversarial test fixtures for the protective hooks — neither `guard_test.go` nor `secretscan_test.go` exists in `pkg/hooks/handlers/`, and no generator integration test verifies that a `yaah generate --agent X` output actually carries the protection set the remit promises (PRAX-2026-05-23-006).

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: The structured session store exists with the right data model (ToolCallRecord, BlockedCalls, FilesModified) but is gated on `input.SessionID != ""` and silently no-ops when the calling agent doesn't propagate one; the SessionLogger writes plaintext lifecycle-only lines to `.claude/logs/sessions.log` with no rotation or schema (PRAX-2026-05-23-007).
